name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
كَذَّبَتْ عَادٌ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٢٣)
```

```english
[The people of] ‘Ad impugned the apostles, (123)
```

```arabic
إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ هُودٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (١٢٤)
```

```english
when Hud, their brother, said to them, ‘Will you not be wary [of Allah]? (124)
```

```arabic
إِنِّى لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ (١٢٥)
```

```english
Indeed I am a trusted apostle [sent] to you. (125)
```

```arabic
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (١٢٦)
```

```english
So be wary of Allah and obey me. (126)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٢٧)
```

```english
I do not ask you any reward for it; my reward lies only with the Lord of all the worlds. (127)
```

```arabic
أَتَبْنُونَ بِكُلِّ رِيعٍ ءَايَةً تَعْبَثُونَ (١٢٨)
```

```english
Do you build futile a sign on every prominence? (128)
```

```arabic
وَتَتَّخِذُونَ مَصَانِعَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَخْلُدُونَ (١٢٩)
```

```english
You set up structures as if you will be immortal, (129)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا بَطَشْتُم بَطَشْتُمْ جَبَّارِينَ (١٣٠)
```

```english
and when you seize [someone for punishment], you seize [him] like tyrants. (130)
```

```arabic
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (١٣١)
```

```english
So be wary of Allah and obey me. (131)
```

```arabic
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَمَدَّكُم بِمَا تَعْلَمُونَ (١٣٢)
```

```english
Be wary of Him who has provided you with whatever you know, (132)
```

```arabic
أَمَدَّكُم بِأَنْعَـٰمٍ وَبَنِينَ (١٣٣)
```

```english
and aided you with sons and with cattle, (133)
```

```arabic
وَجَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (١٣٤)
```

```english
gardens and springs. (134)
```

```arabic
إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٣٥)
```

```english
Indeed I fear for you the punishment of a tremendous day.’ (135)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْنَآ أَوَعَظْتَ أَمْ لَمْ تَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْوَٰعِظِينَ (١٣٦)
```

```english
They said, ‘It is the same to us whether you exhort us or not. (136)
```

```arabic
إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا خُلُقُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٣٧)
```

```english
These are nothing but the traditions of the ancients, (137)
```

```arabic
وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُعَذَّبِينَ (١٣٨)
```

```english
and we will not be punished.’ (138)
```

```arabic
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَهْلَكْنَـٰهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٣٩)
```

```english
So they impugned him, whereupon We destroyed them. There is indeed a sign in that; but most of them do not have faith. (139)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٤٠)
```

```english
Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (140)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses refer to the story of Hud (عليه السلام) and his people, who were the people of ‘Ad.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَذَّبَتْ عادٌ الْمُرْسَلِينَ** **"‘Ad impugned the apostles"** (26:123). The people of ‘Ad were from the first of the "Arabized" Arabs (العرب العاربة الأولى). They inhabited *al-Ahqaf* in the Arabian Peninsula and possessed a sophisticated civilization, fertile lands, and populated dwellings. However, they denied the messengers, were ungrateful for the blessings of Allah, and transgressed. Consequently, Allah destroyed them with a "barren wind" (الريح العقيم) — *a devastating wind that brings no benefit or rain* — ruined their homes, and effaced their traces.

It is said that ‘Ad was the name of their forefather; thus, naming the tribe after him is similar to naming a people after their ancestor, such as saying Tamim, Bakr, or Taghlib, meaning the children of Tamim, Bakr, and Taghlib.

The reason for considering the people as deniers of "the apostles" (المرسلين) — even though they outwardly only denied one of them — has already been discussed in the parallel verse regarding the story of Nuh.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنِّي لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ** **"Indeed I am a trusted apostle to you"** (26:125) — up to His saying — **رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** **"Lord of all the worlds"** (26:127). The discussion regarding these verses has preceded in their counterparts within the story of Nuh (عليه السلام).

Some commentators have mentioned that prefacing these five stories by mentioning the trustworthiness of the messengers, their refusal to ask for a wage for their mission, and their command to the people toward godwariness (التقوى) and obedience (الطاعة) is to highlight that the foundation of the prophetic mission (البعثة) is the call to the knowledge of the Truth and obedience in that which brings the invited closer to reward and distances them from punishment. It also shows that the prophets (عليه السلام) are united upon this, even if they differ in some branches of the various divine laws (الشرائع) which vary according to times and eras, and that they are entirely purified from worldly ambitions.

A similar point applies to the conclusion of all seven stories mentioned in this Surah with His saying: **إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً وَما كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ** **"There is indeed a sign in that, but most of them do not have faith. Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful"** (26:139-140). This indicates that most nations and peoples turn away from the signs of Allah, and that Allah, the Glorified, is All-mighty (عزيز) — *possessing absolute power and invincibility* — and thus He punishes them for their denial, yet He is All-merciful (رحيم) and saves the believers through His mercy. This was previously indicated in the discussion regarding the objective of the Surah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَتَبْنُونَ بِكُلِّ رِيعٍ آيَةً تَعْبَثُونَ** **"Do you build a monument on every high ground vainly?"** (26:128). The *ri‘* (الريع) is a high place on the earth, a *ayah* (الآية) is a mark or monument (العلامة), and *‘abath* (العبث) is an action that has no purposeful end. It seems they used to build structures like landmarks on the peaks of mountains and every elevated ground for the sake of recreation and boasting, without any necessity driving them to do so, but rather out of idle play and following whims (الهوى); thus, he rebuked them for it.

Other meanings have been mentioned for this verse, but they lack evidence from the wording and do not suit the context, so we have set them aside.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَتَتَّخِذُونَ مَصانِعَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَخْلُدُونَ** **"and take up [stately] castles as if you were to live forever?"** (26:129). The *masani‘* (المصانع), according to what has been said, are fortified strongholds, lofty palaces, and high buildings; the singular is *masna‘* (مصنع).

His saying: **لَعَلَّكُمْ تَخْلُدُونَ** **"as if you were to live forever"** is in the position of explaining the cause (التعليل) for what preceded it. That is: you take up these strongholds because you hope for immortality (الخلود); were it not for the hope of immortality, you would not have performed such works which, by their nature, are meant to endure for long ages that the longest of human lifespans cannot fulfill. Other views regarding the meaning of the verse and its individual words have been mentioned, which we have overlooked.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذا بَطَشْتُمْ بَطَشْتُمْ جَبَّارِينَ** **"And when you seize [someone], you seize [him] like tyrants"** (26:130). It is said in *al-Majma‘*: "Seizing (البطش) is oppression through killing with the sword or striking with the whip. The tyrant (الجبار) is one who is exalted over others by his great power. As an attribute of Allah, the Glorified, it is praise, but as an attribute of others, it is blame, because for a servant it means he affects a state of forced compulsion (الجبرية)."

The meaning is: when you manifest intensity in action and prowess, you exceed all bounds in that, just as tyrants exceed bounds in their severity.

The sum of these three verses is that you are extravagant (مسرفون) in both the aspects of desire (الشهوة) and anger (الغضب), transgressing the limits of moderation and departing from the state of servitude (العبودية).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ** **"So be wary of Allah and obey me"** (26:131). This is a derivation (تفريع) from their extravagance in desire and anger and their departure from the state of servitude. Therefore, let them be godwary of Allah and obey him in what he commands them regarding the abandonment of luxury (الإتراف) and arrogance (الاستكبار).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاتَّقُوا الَّذِي أَمَدَّكُمْ بِما تَعْلَمُونَ** **"Be wary of Him who has aided you with what you know"** (26:132) — up to His saying — **وَعُيُونٍ** **"and springs"** (26:134). Al-Raghib said: "The root of *madd* (المد) is pulling or drawing. He said: 'I aided (أمددت) the army with reinforcements (بمدود) and the person with food.' He noted: 'Most often, *imdad* (الإمداد) is used for what is liked, while *madd* (المد) is used for what is disliked.' Allah says: **وَأَمْدَدْناهُمْ بِفاكِهَةٍ** **'and We shall provide them with fruits'** (52:22) and **وَنَمُدُّ لَهُ مِنَ الْعَذابِ مَدًّا** **'and We shall prolong for him the punishment'** (19:79)."

His saying: **وَاتَّقُوا الَّذِي أَمَدَّكُمْ** **"Be wary of Him who has aided you..."** etc., is in the sense of linking a ruling to a description that suggests causality (العلية). That is: be godwary of Allah who aids you with His blessings, for since He aids you with them, it is obligatory upon you to thank Him by placing His blessings in their proper place without luxury or arrogance; for ingratitude toward blessings (كفران النعمة) entails wrath and punishment. Allah says: **لَئِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ وَلَئِنْ كَفَرْتُمْ إِنَّ عَذابِي لَشَدِيدٌ** **"If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you [in blessing], but if you are ungrateful, My punishment is indeed severe"** (14:7).

He mentioned the blessings generally by saying first: **أَمَدَّكُمْ بِما تَعْلَمُونَ** **"He has aided you with what you know,"** then He detailed them by saying second: **أَمَدَّكُمْ بِأَنْعامٍ وَبَنِينَ وَجَنَّاتٍ وَعُيُونٍ** **"He has aided you with cattle and sons, and gardens and springs"** (26:133-134).

In His saying: **أَمَدَّكُمْ بِما تَعْلَمُونَ** **"He has aided you with what you know,"** there is another subtle point (نكتة): that you know these blessings are from His aid and His making, and no one else shares with Him in creating them or providing them. Therefore, He is the One whom you must be godwary of through gratitude and worship (العبادة), rather than idols and statues. Thus, the speech contains a logical proof (الحجة).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ** **"Indeed I fear for you the punishment of a terrible day"** (26:135). This is the reason for the command toward godwariness. That is: I command you toward godwariness out of gratitude because I fear for you the punishment of a great day if you disbelieve and are not grateful. It appears that the "great day" refers to the Day of Resurrection, although some have allowed that it might refer to the day of the punishment of extermination (عذاب الاستئصال).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْنا أَوَعَظْتَ أَمْ لَمْ تَكُنْ مِنَ الْواعِظِينَ** **"They said, ‘It is the same to us whether you advise [us] or do not happen to be one of the advisers’"** (26:136). This is a denial of the effect of his speech and a total despairing of their faith.

It is said: the speech is not devoid of hyperbole (مبالغة). The requirement of the alternative would have been to say: "whether you advise or do not advise." In turning away from that to His saying: **أَمْ لَمْ تَكُنْ مِنَ الْواعِظِينَ** **"or do not happen to be one of the advisers,"** which denies his very status as an adviser, there is an obvious hyperbole.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنْ هذا إِلَّا خُلُقُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** **"This is nothing but the custom of the ancients"** (26:137). The word *khuluq* (الخلق) is with a *damma* on the *kha* and *lam*, or with a *sukun* on the *lam*. Al-Raghib said: "*Khalq* (الخلق) and *khuluq* (الخلق) — with the *kha* having a *fatha* or a *damma* — are originally the same, like *sharb* and *shurb*. However, *khalq* (with *fatha*) is specialized for forms and shapes perceived by sight, while *khuluq* (with *damma*) is specialized for powers and dispositions (السجايا) perceived by insight (البصيرة). Allah says: **إِنَّكَ لَعَلى خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ** **'and indeed you possess a great character'** (68:4). And it is also recited as: **إِنْ هذا إِلَّا خُلُقُ الْأَوَّلِينَ**."

The demonstrative "this" (هذا) refers to what Hud brought, which they called "advice" (وعظ). The meaning is: this call to monotheism (التوحيد) and this advice that you are engaged in is nothing but the habit of the first humans of the past, who were people of myths and superstitions. This is like their saying: **إِنْ هذا إِلَّا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** **"This is nothing but the myths of the ancients"** (6:25).

It is also possible that "this" refers to their own state of polytheism (الشرك) and the worship of deities besides Allah, following their first forefathers, as in their saying: **وَجَدْنا آباءَنا كَذلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ** **"We found our fathers doing so"** (26:74).

Some have suggested the meaning to be: "our creation is nothing but the creation of the ancients; we live as they lived and die as they died, and there is no resurrection, no reckoning, and no punishment." This is far-fetched given the context.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما نَحْنُ بِمُعَذَّبِينَ** **"and we will not be punished"** (26:138). This is a denial of the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection and life after death* — based on the "great day" in the speech of Hud (عليه السلام) referring to the Day of Resurrection.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَهْلَكْناهُمْ إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً** **"So they impugned him, and We destroyed them. There is indeed a sign in that..."** (26:139) — up to His saying — **الرَّحِيمُ** **"the All-merciful"** (26:140). Its meaning is clear from what has preceded.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the book *Kamal al-Din* and *Rawdat al-Kafi*, it is narrated with a chain of transmission from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali, from Abu Ja‘far Muhammad bin ‘Ali al-Baqir (عليه السلام) in a tradition: "And Nuh said: 'Indeed Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, is sending a prophet called Hud. He will call his people to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, but they will deny him. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, will destroy them with the wind. So whoever among you reaches him, let him believe in him and follow him, for Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, will save him from the punishment of the wind.'"

"And Nuh commanded his son Shem to observe this testament at the beginning of every year, and it would be a day of celebration (عيد) for them, wherein they would observe the sending of Hud and the time in which he would appear."

"When Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, sent Hud, they looked into the knowledge and faith they possessed, the inheritance of knowledge, the Greatest Name (الاسم الأكبر), and the traces of the knowledge of prophethood. They found Hud to be a prophet whom their father Nuh had given glad tidings of. So they believed in him, testified to his truth, and followed him, and they were saved from the punishment of the wind. This is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَإِلى عادٍ أَخاهُمْ هُوداً** **'And to ‘Ad [We sent] Hud, their brother'** (7:65) and His saying: **كَذَّبَتْ عادٌ الْمُرْسَلِينَ ـ إِذْ قالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ هُودٌ أَلا تَتَّقُونَ** **'‘Ad impugned the apostles, when Hud, their brother, said to them, "Will you not be wary [of Allah]?"'** (26:123-124)."

In *al-Majma‘*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **آيَةً تَعْبَثُونَ** **"a monument vainly"**: "Meaning that which you do not need for your dwelling, but you only intend vanity, play, and diversion by it. It is as if he considered their building of what they could do without as vanity on their part." This is from Ibn ‘Abbas in the narration of ‘Ata’. It is supported by the reported tradition from Anas bin Malik: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) went out and saw a dome (قبة) and said: 'What is this?' His companions said to him: 'This belongs to a man from the Ansar.' He remained until the owner came and greeted the people, but the Prophet turned away from him. He did this several times until the man recognized the anger and the turning away."

"He complained about this to his companions and said: 'By Allah, I find the look of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) strange; I do not know what has happened regarding me or what I have done?' They said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) went out and saw your dome and asked: "To whom does this belong?" and we informed him.' So the man returned to his dome and leveled it to the ground. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) went out one day and did not see the dome, so he said: 'What happened to the dome that was here?' They said: 'Its owner complained to us of your turning away from him, so we informed him and he demolished it.'"

"He said: 'Indeed, everything that is built is a burden (وبال) upon its owner on the Day of Resurrection, except that which is indispensable.'"

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذا بَطَشْتُمْ بَطَشْتُمْ جَبَّارِينَ** **"And when you seize [someone], you seize [him] like tyrants"**, he said: "You kill out of anger without justification."
